paragraphs.body: Problem Players

annotations.comment: Interesting title and I do love a journal set up.

paragraphs.body: Session Notes:

Wanted to just start the game already, but Angie insisted I do this session 0 thing. They gave me a list of their “reds and yellows,” things that absolutely terrify them, and then things that are just a little scary. Talked to them about the world I’m putting them in. It’s sort of a high fantasy, high stakes, high horror setting, so I’m glad I can at least keep those fears in mind for them.

annotations.comment: I love seeing a gaming plot in a story. Nice.
annotations.context_left: is session 0 thing. They gave me a list of their “reds and yellows,” things that absolutely terrify
annotations.context_right: , and then things that are just a little scary. Talked to them about the world I’m putting them in.

paragraphs.body: Kevin wanted to do this weird divination theme with his wizard. Since a lot of the campaign is going to deal with horror, I told him it didn’t really fit into the world. Worked with him, and now he’s playing a human fighter barbarian (real original). He seemed okay with it. Angie’s playing a water genasi druid, Wyatt wants to play a half-orc barbarian, and Julian’s trying to choose between a tiefling bard or a tiefling Warlock (super original).

annotations.comment: Oh, I love the cattiness here. It’s really subtle but we get the sense right away that this speaker/writer is a little over this work.

paragraphs.body: Session 1:

First session was a bit stressful. I had a lot I wanted to get done, but they got really distracted talking to each other – especially Wyatt and Julian. They were barely even in character most of the time.

annotations.comment: Nice. It sounds like they’re being friends and that annoys him, which is funny!

paragraphs.body: Somehow, I managed to gently nudge them into the bar where they were supposed to get a quest from the bartender. After some more shenanigans, Kevin talked to the bartender and learned about the recent string of missing children in town. I’ve been working on this plot-hook for a while, and it’s going to lead to all sorts of crazy twists. I even have my PC from another game that is going to help rescue them at some point. I’m really excited.

annotations.comment: I would love to get more of a sense of whether they were interested in the missing kids or not.

paragraphs.body: After that, they started investigating the town and found this lead-shooter store. I decided to tease this super powerful lead-shooter that cost a thousand gold pieces so they would have something to work towards. Angie started making plans to steal it and egging everyone else on, and I could tell Julian was starting to get excited about stealing it too. This lead-shooter isn’t supposed to come in until the third section of the campaign when they return to the town it all began, so I told them that the gun store was kind of a joke and not real. Bummed I had to scrap it, but at least my story won’t be ruined. Anyway, that’s where that session ended off. I need them to stop derailing everything.

annotations.text: need them to stop derailing everything.
annotations.comment: I think you can hit this a little harder — his (?) annoyance at their being autonomous individuals who won’t play the game as he prefers is a great idea, so let’s see that. He might even recount what was said.

paragraphs.body: Session 2:

Angie canceled last week – some family emergency or something. It wasn’t too big a deal. I mean, this next session wasn’t really supposed to be about her anyway, but no one else wanted to show up, so I guess we’ve got to cater to everyone.

We met up the next week and Kevin led the investigation into the abandoned mineshaft for the next section of the story. Together with my guide NPC, Hector Strongstone, they fought off mobs of the undead. Hector did a lot of work for the party, holding the mass of zombies and skeletons off while the players picked them off from afar. At one point, Hector even started giving orders to the other players like some sort of bad-ass general! Everyone was complaining they didn’t get to do much, but Kevin had that moment of finding and activating that trap that collapsed the undead section of the tunnel, so they should be happy.

annotations.comment: This week?Love that they don’t really like this but that he isn’t asking them, either.

paragraphs.body: People were a bit quiet after the encounter, and I figured they wanted more roleplay. Kevin really leaped at this and started encouraging the party to soldier on. Wyatt wanted to slow everything down though with this really awkward confession to Julian’s bard. I tried to quickly brush past it, but they insisted on staying in the scene. Eventually, I had to put on my dungeon master hat and tell them to knock it off. Was able to set up next week’s session before we ran out of time. There’s these cages that swoop down from the tunnel’s ceiling and capture Angie and Julian!

annotations.comment: Can you give us a little moment that sets up Angie’s quitting. What she says, maybe?
annotations.context_left: y to soldier on. Wyatt wanted to slow everything down though with this really awkward confession to
annotations.context_right: ’s bard. I tried to quickly brush past it, but they insisted on staying in the scene. Eventually, I

paragraphs.body: Session 3:

Well, with Angie quitting, it was actually much easier to wrangle everyone else and make them really get into my story. This session was supposed to be a two-person rescue mission, but with Angie gone, I had to improvise. Instead, I just had the cages swoop down and capture Julian and Wyatt. Now it was just Kevin, who’s much more respectful to my story anyway. I told him that he recognized the cages and that he knew he could find them at the end of the dungeon.

annotations.comment: Love that he doesn’t get his eventual doom has been set.

paragraphs.body: Wyatt kept trying to bring the scene over to him and Julian trapped in the cages. I let them have a bit of conversation, but I didn’t really want to linger on them because I wanted to make Kevin feel a bit isolated. It was time to really push the horror parts of the campaign.

He ventured through the dungeon a bit, fighting off giant spiders and struggling to see in the darkness. I’m really glad I made him switch from his wizard class because, without spells and a character with Darkvision, he really struggled in this part. Also, without Angie, there was no one to help him, so we really got to define the core atmosphere of the campaign. We had to stop for time halfway through his little dungeon crawl, so we’ll finish it next week.

annotations.comment: Nice, but again, a little more — help us see how Wyatt is complaining and that Kevin is scared.

paragraphs.body: Session 4:

Finally got everyone back in to play. I feel like I’m the only one who has been trying to schedule these sessions anymore. Julian and Wyatt had reached out to me outside of the game (after canceling two weeks in a row) and asked if they could have a moment in the cages together. I said sure, whatever, and we started the session with them. I could tell it was going to get into the sappy territory, so I reminded them that one of my yellow cards (that’s right, I matter too) was romance scenes. They had to wrap it up pretty quickly and we got to move back into the actual story with Kevin.

With Kevin, things really escalated in the mineshaft. Kevin mentioned in one of his red cards that he’s really uncomfortable with drowning. I think it had something to do with one of his family members in the past. Anyway, there was this giant room that had flooded from constant rainwater. He had to navigate through it, but, the thing is, he didn’t even have to go into the water! I mean sure there were wind mephitis that could push him around, but if he had stuck to the clues I had given, he would have seen that there was a way to drain the room entirely. Instead, he just bumbled around, fell into the water, and everyone threw a fit when he suddenly couldn’t swim in full-plate armor.

annotations.comment: I really appreciate this, Cameron — I love the comparison of the narrator’s holding of his yellow cards with his complete obliviousness that he’s setting off one of Kevin’s. Very nicely done.

paragraphs.body: Julian and Wyatt started complaining (of course) and told Kevin that he didn’t have to play out the scene if he didn’t want to. They antagonized him enough that he actually took their side, telling me it wasn’t cool to do that. I told them that if they hated my style of DM’ing so much, they could write a campaign themselves. That shut them up.

Kevin made it to the cages and freed everyone. I didn’t feel like running the encounter there, so it was pretty easy. We ended the session early. I’ve been reaching out, trying to put the next session together, but no one’s responded yet. Whatever, we’ll see. I think in the next tunnel, I’m going to put more flooded rooms in.

annotations.comment: Nice. I wanted more stakes for him here — the parents hadn’t paid yet or whatever — so we can see that he has doomed himself as a DM. Overall, this is a smart and strong draft and about a situation that’s not written about enough. What I would suggest for a revision is allowing us glimpses into what happens in these meetings with more detail. I could imagine this narrator writing down what Angie says, for example, without even realizing that she’s telling him, hey, I’m out. I also suggest giving the narrator more stakes — a loss of income or reputation or something — just so the ending hits a bit harder when we realize (even if he doesn’t) that he’s ruined things for himself. Great work! Happy revising! (late)
